content.problemStatement:
Little Chef loves mathematics.Every day, he solves some mathematical problems to improve his skill.



A few days ago, he found a popular problem about turtles. The problem is as follows. Little Chef is given **R** rectangles, numbered 1 through **R**. The width and height of the **i**-th rectangle are **Ni** and **Mi** unit respectively. There is a turtle located on the top-left corner of each rectangle. For each rectangle, count the number of ways the turtle can reach the bottom-right corner, if each turtle can only move right or down 1 unit at any time.The turtle is not allowed to move outside the rectangle, but, of course, the turtle can move on the boundary of the rectangle.



In less than one second, this problem was solved for all rectangles.He felt that the problem was too easy. This morning, Little Chef wanted more challenges. Thus, for each rectangle **i**, he cut and removed a rectangle of **Ai** × **Bi** unit from the top-right corner. See the following figure for detail.



He could not solve this new version of the problem easily. Help him count the number of ways each turtle can reach the bottom-right corner using the same rule as before.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer R (1 ≤ R ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next R lines contains four space‑separated integers Ni, Mi, Ai, Bi describing a rectangle of width Ni and height Mi (both measured in unit squares) from which a rectangle of size Ai × Bi has been removed from the top‑right corner. It is guaranteed that 2 ≤ Ni, Mi ≤ 400000 and 1 ≤ Ai < Ni, 1 ≤ Bi < Mi.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of monotonic lattice paths (moving only right or down by one unit) that start at the top‑left corner of the original rectangle and end at its bottom‑right corner while never entering the removed top‑right sub‑rectangle. Print the answer modulo 1 000 000 007.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There are 5 ways the turtle can reach the bottom-right corner: down-right, right-down, down-down-right, right-down-down, and down-right-down.
There are 35 ways the turtle can reach the bottom-right corner.
There are 126 ways the turtle can reach the bottom-right corner.

solution.approach:
For a rectangle of size Ni × Mi the turtle needs (Ni‑1) right moves and (Mi‑1) down moves. Without any hole the number of monotone paths is C(Ni+Mi‑2, Ni‑1). The removed part is a rectangle of width Ai and height Bi placed at the top‑right corner. Any path that enters the forbidden area must cross the vertical line x = Ni‑Ai at a row y < Bi. Instead of counting forbidden paths directly we count the allowed ones.

A path is allowed iff it reaches the vertical line x = Ni‑Ai **after** it has already moved down at least Bi steps. Let j be the row (0‑based) where the path first steps onto column Ni‑Ai. The row j must satisfy Bi ≤ j ≤ Mi‑1. The path can be split into two independent parts:
1. From (0,0) to (Ni‑Ai‑1, j) – this needs (Ni‑Ai‑1) right moves and j down moves, giving C((Ni‑Ai‑1)+j, Ni‑Ai‑1) possibilities.
2. From (Ni‑Ai, j) to the target (Ni‑1, Mi‑1) – this needs Ai right moves and (Mi‑1‑j) down moves, giving C(Ai + (Mi‑1‑j), Ai) possibilities.

Summing over all admissible j yields the answer:

answer = Σ_{j=Bi}^{Mi‑1} C(Ni‑Ai‑1 + j, Ni‑Ai‑1) · C(Ai + Mi‑1‑j, Ai)   (mod MOD)

All binomial coefficients are computed modulo MOD = 1 000 000 007 using pre‑computed factorials and modular inverses. The maximum value of n in any C(n,k) is at most (max Ni + max Mi) ≤ 800 000, so a single O(N) pre‑computation suffices for all test cases.

Time Complexity: O(maxN) for the pre‑computation plus O( Σ (Mi‑Bi) ) for answering all queries (worst‑case ≤ 4·10⁶ operations, well within limits).
Space Complexity: O(maxN) for factorial and inverse arrays.
